questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe computational social choice? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It lies at the intersection of algorithms, game theory, and social choice
It studies how groups of agents make collective decisions in a principled way
It ignores paradoxes and impossibility results because those belong only to political science
It investigates how computational complexity affects feasibility and strategic manipulation
It analyses how voting rules aggregate individual preferences into a single outcome

explanations.text: This is the opening characterisation of the field in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Field at the intersection of algorithms, game theory, and social choice

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state this as a central aim of the field.
explanations.notes_quote: Studies how groups of agents make collective decisions in a principled way

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: computational social choice explicitly explores paradoxes and impossibility results such as Arrow and Gibbard-Satterthwaite.
Complexity is presented both as a feasibility issue and as a possible protection against manipulation.
explanations.notes_quote: Investigates how computational complexity affects feasibility

explanations.text: This is one of the core tasks listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Analyses how voting rules aggregate individual preferences into a single outcome

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe applications of voting theory beyond political elections? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Recommender systems can aggregate user ratings via voting rules to represent diverse tastes
Participatory budgeting and project selection are examples of resource-allocation applications
Voting theory is irrelevant to multi-agent AI because agents cannot vote over actions or plans
Multiple agents or models can vote over actions or plans in distributed AI settings
The notes restrict applications entirely to national elections and referenda

explanations.text: This is the recommender-systems application listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Recommender systems: aggregate user ratings via voting rules to represent diverse tastes

explanations.text: The resource-allocation application is described exactly this way.
explanations.notes_quote: Resource allocation: choose subsets of projects in participatory budgeting; allocate resources fairly

explanations.text: The notes explicitly include multi-agent AI as an application area where agents or models vote over actions or plans.
This is the multi-agent AI application given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Multi-agent AI: multiple agents/models "vote" over actions/plans; coordinate swarms, robot teams, distributed AI

explanations.text: The section is specifically titled 'Applications Beyond Political Elections' and lists several non-political uses.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.1: Setting; Section 10.1: Preference Formats; Section 10.1: Outcome Formats
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the formal setting, preference formats, and outcome formats introduced in Week 10? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The set of alternatives is denoted $C = \{c_1, \ldots, c_m\}$ and the set of voters is $N = \{1, \ldots, n\}$
A preference profile $R$ is the collection of all voters' preferences
The notes allow rankings, approval sets, and numerical scores as preference formats
A social welfare function outputs one winning alternative only
Multi-winner rules output a committee or set of $k$ winners

explanations.text: These are the formal symbols introduced in the setting.
explanations.notes_quote: Set of alternatives (candidates): $C = \{c_1, \ldots, c_m\}$; Set of voters (agents): $N = \{1, \ldots, n\}$

explanations.text: The notes define the preference profile exactly this way.
explanations.notes_quote: Preference profile $R$: collection of all voters' preferences

explanations.text: All three formats appear in the preference-format table.
explanations.notes_quote: Rankings ... Approval sets ... Scores/stars

explanations.text: A social welfare function outputs a complete ranking of all alternatives, not just a single winner. Single-winner rules are social choice functions.
This is the outcome-format entry for multi-winner rules.
explanations.notes_quote: Multi-winner rules | Committee or set of $k$ winners

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the formal model for single-winner voting rules? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathcal{L}(C)$ denotes the set of all strict linear orders on $C$
A profile is written $R = (R_1, \ldots, R_n) \in \mathcal{L}(C)^n$
A voting rule is a function $F: \mathcal{L}(C)^n \to 2^C \setminus \{\emptyset\}$
A rule is resolute if it always returns every candidate tied for first place
Most rules are described as irresolute and therefore need tie-breaking

explanations.text: This is the notation introduced in the formal model.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathcal{L}(C)$: set of all strict linear orders on $C$

explanations.text: The notes define the profile exactly in this form.
explanations.notes_quote: Each voter $i$ supplies ballot $R_i$, giving profile $R = (R_1, \ldots, R_n) \in \mathcal{L}(C)^n$

explanations.text: This is the formal type signature of a voting rule in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Voting rule: $F: \mathcal{L}(C)^n \to 2^C \setminus \{\emptyset\}$

explanations.text: Resolute means $|F(R)| = 1$ for all profiles, not that all tied winners are returned.
The notes explicitly say most rules are irresolute and need tie-breaking.
explanations.notes_quote: most rules are irresolute (need tie-breaking)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe plurality voting? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Plurality elects the alternative ranked first most often
Its scoring vector is $(1, 0, \ldots, 0)$
The notes describe plurality as anonymous and neutral
For 3 or more candidates, plurality always elects the Condorcet winner whenever one exists
For 3 or more candidates, plurality may elect a candidate without majority support

explanations.text: This is the definition of plurality in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Elect the alternative ranked first most often

explanations.text: The scoring vector is stated explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: Scoring vector $(1, 0, \ldots, 0)$

explanations.text: These two properties are listed directly under plurality.
explanations.notes_quote: Anonymous and neutral

explanations.text: The notes say plurality can fail badly for 3 or more candidates, even electing a Condorcet loser.
This is one of the limitations highlighted in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: For 3+ candidates: winner may lack majority support

questions.text:
Using the worked profile below, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

| 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| a | a | b | c | d |
| b | b | c | b | b |
| c | c | d | d | c |
| d | d | a | a | a |

options.text: Under plurality, the scores are $a=2$, $b=1$, $c=1$, $d=1$, so $a$ wins
In the same example, voters 3, 4, and 5 rank $a$ last
Candidate $b$ is ranked first or second by every voter and beats $a$ head-to-head 3-2
Under Borda count on this profile, $a$ is the winner with 11 points
Under Borda count on this profile, the scores are $a=6$, $b=11$, $c=8$, $d=5$, so $b$ wins

explanations.text: These are the plurality tallies given in the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: Plurality: a=2, b=1, c=1, d=1. Winner: a

explanations.text: The notes explicitly point this out to show plurality's weakness.
explanations.notes_quote: But voters 3,4,5 rank a last

explanations.text: This is the head-to-head comparison highlighted in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: B is ranked 1st or 2nd by everyone and beats a head-to-head 3-2

explanations.text: Under Borda, $b$ wins with 11 points; $a$ has only 6.
These are the Borda totals reported in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Same example: a=6, b=11, c=8, d=5. Borda winner: b

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe positional scoring rules (PSRs)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A PSR is defined by a scoring vector $s = (s_1, \ldots, s_m)$ with $s_1 \geq s_2 \geq \ldots \geq s_m$ and $s_1 > s_m$
Plurality, Borda, veto, and $k$-approval are all examples of PSRs
Veto uses the scoring vector $(1, 1, \ldots, 1, 0)$
$k$-approval uses the scoring vector $(k, k-1, \ldots, 1, 0, \ldots, 0)$
Borda uses the scoring vector $(m-1, m-2, \ldots, 0)$

explanations.text: This is the formal PSR definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Defined by scoring vector $s = (s_1, \ldots, s_m)$ with $s_1 \geq s_2 \geq \ldots \geq s_m$ and $s_1 > s_m$

explanations.text: All four rules appear in the PSR table.
explanations.notes_quote: Borda ... Plurality ... Veto ... $k$-approval

explanations.text: This is the veto scoring vector listed in the table.
$k$-approval gives 1 point to the top $k$ positions and 0 thereafter; it does not use descending scores $k, k-1, \ldots$.
This is the Borda scoring vector in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Borda | $(m-1, m-2, \ldots, 0)$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe plurality with runoff and IRV? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Plurality with runoff sends the top two plurality candidates to a majority runoff
IRV repeatedly eliminates the candidate with the fewest first-place votes until one candidate remains
The notes list Ireland, Australia, Maine, Alaska, and NYC as places using IRV
IRV is identical to Borda count whenever there are at least four candidates
The notes present IRV as an elimination-based method

explanations.text: This is the definition given for plurality with runoff.
explanations.notes_quote: Plurality with runoff: Top two from plurality go to a majority runoff

explanations.text: This is the IRV procedure stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Repeatedly eliminate the candidate with fewest first-place votes; last standing wins

explanations.text: These jurisdictions are listed explicitly in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Used in Ireland, Australia, Maine, Alaska, NYC

explanations.text: IRV and Borda are different rules with different winner computations; the notes do not identify them as equivalent.
IRV appears under the elimination-based methods heading.
explanations.notes_quote: Elimination-Based Methods

questions.text: Using the IRV worked example on the same profile, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In round 1, the first-place counts are $a=2$, $b=1$, $c=1$, $d=1$
Candidate $b$ is eliminated first by alphabetical tie-break
After eliminating $b$, round 2 gives $a=2$, $c=2$, $d=1$
The final IRV winner is $c$
The example shows IRV electing the Condorcet winner $b$

explanations.text: These are the round-1 counts listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 1: a=2, b=1, c=1, d=1

explanations.text: The notes specify that $b$ is eliminated first by alphabetical tie-break.
explanations.notes_quote: Eliminate b (alphabetical tie-break)

explanations.text: These are the round-2 counts after removing $b$.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 2 (without b): a=2, c=2, d=1

explanations.text: The notes conclude with $c$ as the IRV winner.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 3: a=2, c=3. Winner: c

explanations.text: The notes say IRV eliminated $b$, who was the Condorcet winner, so IRV failed the Condorcet criterion here.
explanations.notes_quote: IRV eliminated b, who was the Condorcet winner

questions.source_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet's Paradox; Section 10.3: Condorcet Winner
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Condorcet's paradox and the Condorcet winner concept? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Condorcet's paradox shows that majority preferences can be cyclical
A Condorcet winner defeats every other candidate in pairwise majority comparison
The Condorcet criterion requires electing the Condorcet winner whenever one exists
Condorcet's paradox implies that pairwise majority comparisons are always transitive
The notes illustrate the paradox with 3 voters and 3 alternatives having cyclically shifted rankings

explanations.text: This is the core content of Condorcet's paradox in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Majority preferences can be cyclical: $a \succ b \succ c \succ a$

explanations.text: This is the definition of a Condorcet winner.
explanations.notes_quote: A Condorcet winner defeats every other candidate in pairwise majority comparison

explanations.text: This is the Condorcet criterion as stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: The Condorcet criterion requires electing the Condorcet winner whenever one exists

explanations.text: The paradox shows the opposite: majority preferences can fail to be transitive and can cycle.
This is the example format mentioned in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Example: 3 voters, 3 alternatives with cyclically shifted rankings

questions.source_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Condorcet consistency of common voting rules? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: No positional scoring rule is Condorcet-consistent for $m \geq 3$
Plurality, Borda, and IRV are all given as examples of rules that fail Condorcet consistency
Borda is Condorcet-consistent whenever there are exactly four candidates
The notes say the failure of PSRs is proven for any scoring vector
IRV can fail Condorcet consistency because it may eliminate the Condorcet winner before the final round

explanations.text: This is the headline theorem stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules
explanations.notes_quote: No PSR is Condorcet-consistent (for $m \geq 3$)

explanations.text: These three rules are explicitly listed as failing Condorcet consistency.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules
explanations.notes_quote: Plurality, Borda, IRV all fail Condorcet consistency

explanations.text: The notes say no PSR is Condorcet-consistent for $m \geq 3$, so Borda is not an exception at four candidates.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the non-Condorcet-consistency result is proven for any scoring vector.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules
explanations.notes_quote: proven for any scoring vector

explanations.text: This is exactly what happened in the IRV worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: IRV eliminated b, who was the Condorcet winner

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Copeland's rule, maximin, and Dodgson's rule? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Copeland's rule gives +1 for each pairwise win and +0.5 for each tie
A Condorcet winner under Copeland's rule gets score $m-1$ and is uniquely maximal
Maximin elects the candidate whose minimum pairwise support is highest
Dodgson's score is the maximum number of adjacent swaps needed to become a Condorcet winner
The notes state that computing Dodgson's rule is NP-hard

explanations.text: This is the definition of Copeland's rule in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: +1 per pairwise win, +0.5 per tie

explanations.text: This is the reason Copeland is Condorcet-consistent.
explanations.notes_quote: Condorcet winner scores $m-1$ (uniquely maximal)

explanations.text: This is the maximin definition given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: For each candidate, find minimum pairwise support; Elect candidate with highest minimum

explanations.text: Dodgson's score is the minimum, not maximum, number of adjacent swaps needed to become a Condorcet winner.
The computational hardness of Dodgson's rule is stated explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: NP-hard to compute

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the worked examples for Copeland and Dodgson? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the Copeland example, the scores are $A=0$, $B=3$, $C=2$, $D=1$
The Copeland winner in the example is $B$
In the Dodgson example, candidate $B$ needs 3 adjacent swaps to become a Condorcet winner
The notes give Dodgson score 0 to $B$ in the example because $B$ is already the Condorcet winner
The notes specify the three swaps as swapping $B$ with $C$ in voter 4, $B$ with $D$ in voter 4, and $A$ with $B$ in voter 1

explanations.text: These are the Copeland scores reported in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Copeland Example: A=0, B=3, C=2, D=1

explanations.text: The notes identify $B$ as the Copeland winner.
explanations.notes_quote: Winner: B

explanations.text: This is the Dodgson score given in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: B needs 3 swaps ... Dodgson score of B = 3

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say $B$ needs 3 swaps, so its Dodgson score is 3, not 0.
These are the exact swaps listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: swap B-C in voter 4, B-D in voter 4, A-B in voter 1

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe strategic manipulation and strategyproofness? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A ballot need not equal a voter's true preference, because a voter may misreport
A rule is strategyproof if no voter can benefit by submitting an untruthful ballot
The formal definition compares the truthful outcome $F(R)$ with the manipulated outcome $F(R'_i, R_{-i})$ according to voter $i$'s true ranking $R_i$
A rule is strategyproof whenever every voter has the same top-ranked candidate
The notes distinguish explicitly between a ballot and a true preference

explanations.text: This distinction is the starting point of the manipulation section.
explanations.notes_quote: Ballot $\neq$ true preference: voter may misreport

explanations.text: This is the informal content of the formal strategyproofness definition.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is strategyproof if no voter ... and untruthful ballot $R'_i$ exist such that $R_i$ ranks $F(R'_i, R_{-i})$ above $F(R)$

explanations.text: The formal definition is stated exactly in these terms.
explanations.notes_quote: $R_i$ ranks $F(R'_i, R_{-i})$ above $F(R)$

explanations.text: Strategyproofness is a property of the rule across all profiles, not a condition that only concerns unanimous top choices.
The notes explicitly emphasise this distinction.
explanations.notes_quote: Ballot $\neq$ true preference

questions.source_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the manipulation examples in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the Borda manipulation example, voter 3 changes from $a \succ b \succ c \succ d$ to $a \succ c \succ d \succ b$
That Borda manipulation lowers $b$'s score from 8 to 6 and makes $a$ win
Plurality is strategyproof for 2 alternatives but not for 3 or more
The plurality manipulation idea described is to vote for a second choice to prevent a third choice from winning
The notes claim Borda is strategyproof because point totals reveal all manipulations immediately

explanations.text: This is the exact misreport given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples
explanations.notes_quote: Voter 3 (true: $a \succ b \succ c \succ d$) changes to $a \succ c \succ d \succ b$

explanations.text: The notes explain the effect of the manipulation in exactly these terms.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples
explanations.notes_quote: dropping b from 8 to 6 points, making a win instead

explanations.text: This is the plurality statement in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples
explanations.notes_quote: Plurality: Strategyproof for 2 alternatives, not for 3+

explanations.text: This is the strategic intuition given for plurality with 3 or more alternatives.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples
explanations.notes_quote: vote for second choice to prevent third choice winning

explanations.text: The notes present Borda as manipulable via the worked example, not strategyproof.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Manipulation Examples

questions.source_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem and its definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Onto (surjective) means every candidate can win under some profile
Dictatorial means one voter's top choice always wins
For $m \geq 3$, a resolute SCF is strategyproof and onto if and only if it is a dictatorship
The theorem implies every non-dictatorial onto rule with at least 3 candidates is manipulable
The theorem states that every anonymous rule is strategyproof

explanations.text: This is the definition of onto in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Onto (surjective): every candidate can win under some profile

explanations.text: This is the definition of dictatorship given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Dictatorial: one voter's top choice always wins

explanations.text: This is the theorem statement exactly as written.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Let $m \geq 3$. A resolute SCF $F$ is strategyproof and onto $C$ if and only if $F$ is a dictatorship

explanations.text: This is the immediate implication drawn in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Any non-dictatorial, onto voting rule with $m \geq 3$ is manipulable

explanations.text: The theorem does not say anonymous rules are strategyproof; in fact, it shows strong impossibility for non-dictatorial onto rules.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem

questions.source_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the proof sketch of Gibbard-Satterthwaite given in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: One step proves strong monotonicity: if $F(R)=a$, then preserving $a$ above others keeps $a$ the winner
Another step proves unanimity: if all voters rank $a$ above $b$, then an SP+onto rule cannot output $b$
The proof sketch assumes neutrality and $m \geq n$
The proof sketch concludes voter 1 must be a dictator via neutrality after constructing a symmetric cyclic profile
The proof sketch relies on Condorcet consistency rather than strategyproofness

explanations.text: This is the first numbered step in the proof sketch.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Strong monotonicity: If $F$ is SP and $F(R)=a$, then $F(R')=a$ for all $R'$ preserving $a$'s relative position above others

explanations.text: This is the second numbered step in the proof sketch.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Unanimity: If all voters rank $a$ above $b$ and $F$ is SP+onto, then $F(R) \neq b$

explanations.text: These assumptions are stated in the proof sketch heading.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Proof sketch (with neutrality, $m \geq n$)

explanations.text: This is the final move described in the proof sketch.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: show voter 1 must be a dictator via neutrality

explanations.text: The proof sketch is built around strategyproofness, onto-ness, neutrality, strong monotonicity, and unanimity, not Condorcet consistency.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem

questions.source_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the barriers to manipulation discussed in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Under single-peaked preferences, each voter's preference decreases monotonically away from their peak on a line
For odd $n$, single-peaked preferences guarantee a Condorcet winner at the median peak
Black's Median Voter Theorem states that under single-peaked preferences and odd $n$, the Condorcet winner equals the median peak
If the $F$-manipulation problem is NP-hard, the rule is called resistant to manipulation
Single-peaked preferences make Condorcet cycles more likely because voters cluster around extremes

explanations.text: This is the definition of single-peakedness given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: Profile is single-peaked w.r.t. ordering $\gg$ if each voter's preference decreases monotonically away from their peak on the line

explanations.text: This is one of the consequences listed for single-peaked preferences.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: Condorcet winner always exists (odd $n$) = candidate at median peak

explanations.text: This is Black's Median Voter Theorem as stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: Under single-peaked preferences (odd $n$), Condorcet winner exists and equals the median peak

explanations.text: This is the computational-barrier definition in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: If the $F$-manipulation problem ... is NP-hard, then $F$ is resistant to manipulation

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: under single-peaked preferences, Condorcet cycles cannot occur.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation

questions.text:
Which of the following correctly describe Exercise 1 and its computational task? (Select ALL that apply.)

| 33 | 16 | 3 | 8 | 18 | 22 |
|----|-----|---|---|-----|-----|
| a | b | c | c | d | e |
| b | d | d | e | e | c |
| c | c | b | b | c | b |
| d | e | a | d | b | d |
| e | a | e | a | a | a |

options.text: The profile contains 100 voters in total
The exercise asks for winners under plurality, Borda, Llull's, Dodgson's, maximin, and IRV
The exercise is purely theoretical and does not ask for any winner computations
The alternatives appearing in the profile are $a,b,c,d,e$
The six voter blocks have sizes 33, 16, 3, 8, 18, and 22

explanations.text: Summing the six voter blocks gives 100, matching the exercise title.
explanations.notes_quote: Exercise 1: 100-Voter Profile

explanations.text: These are exactly the rules listed in the exercise prompt.
explanations.notes_quote: Compute winners under plurality, Borda, Llull's, Dodgson's, maximin, and IRV

explanations.text: The exercise explicitly asks for winner computations under six different rules.
Those are the five alternatives shown in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: | a | b | c | c | d | e |

explanations.text: These are the six block sizes in the profile header.

questions.source_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the anonymity-neutrality impossibility exercise? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The profile considered has voter 1 ranking $a \succ b$ and voter 2 ranking $b \succ a$
Anonymity means swapping the voters does not change the outcome
Neutrality means swapping $a$ and $b$ in all rankings should swap the winner
The contradiction arises because the profile is symmetric under both operations
The exercise concludes that anonymity and neutrality together imply dictatorship

explanations.text: This is the exact profile used in the exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)
explanations.notes_quote: With voter 1: $a \succ b$, voter 2: $b \succ a$

explanations.text: This is the definition of anonymity used in the exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)
explanations.notes_quote: Anonymity: swapping voters doesn't change outcome

explanations.text: This is the neutrality condition stated in the exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)
explanations.notes_quote: Neutrality: swapping $a \leftrightarrow b$ in all rankings should swap the winner

explanations.text: This symmetry is exactly what yields the contradiction in the exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)
explanations.notes_quote: the profile is symmetric under both operations -- contradiction

explanations.text: The exercise establishes a contradiction for anonymity plus neutrality in this setting; it does not conclude dictatorship.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 2: Impossibility of Anonymous + Neutral (2 voters, 2 alternatives)

questions.source_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 3: No PSR is Condorcet-Consistent (m >= 3); Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 4: Approval Voting is Non-Manipulable
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Exercises 3 and 4? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Exercise 3 splits into the cases $s_2 = 0$ and $s_2 > 0$
When $s_2 = 0$, Exercise 3 identifies the rule as plurality
When $s_2 > 0$, Exercise 3 says to modify the Borda counterexample
Exercise 4 argues approval voting is non-manipulable because changing approvals can only help less-preferred candidates or hurt preferred ones
Exercise 4 proves approval voting is manipulable whenever there are at least three candidates

explanations.text: These are the two cases listed in Exercise 3.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 3: No PSR is Condorcet-Consistent (m >= 3)
explanations.notes_quote: Case $s_2 = 0$ ... Case $s_2 > 0$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify the $s_2=0$ case with plurality.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 3: No PSR is Condorcet-Consistent (m >= 3)
explanations.notes_quote: Case $s_2 = 0$: this is plurality

explanations.text: This is the instruction given for the second case.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 3: No PSR is Condorcet-Consistent (m >= 3)
explanations.notes_quote: Case $s_2 > 0$: modify the Borda counterexample

explanations.text: This is the reasoning given in Exercise 4.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 4: Approval Voting is Non-Manipulable
explanations.notes_quote: approving a less-preferred candidate only helps that candidate; removing approval from a preferred candidate only hurts them

explanations.text: The notes state the opposite in Exercise 4: approval voting is non-manipulable.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises (SGT10): Exercise 4: Approval Voting is Non-Manipulable

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match voting-rule terms with their definitions from the key definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Plurality: PSR $(1,0,\ldots,0)$ based on top-choice count
Borda count: PSR $(m-1, m-2, \ldots, 0)$
IRV/STV: iteratively eliminate the lowest-plurality candidate
Copeland: minimum adjacent swaps to become Condorcet winner
Dodgson: minimum swaps to become Condorcet winner; NP-hard

explanations.text: This is the plurality entry in the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Plurality | PSR $(1,0,\ldots,0)$; top-choice count

explanations.text: This is the Borda entry in the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Borda count | PSR $(m-1, m-2, \ldots, 0)$

explanations.text: This is the IRV/STV entry in the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: IRV/STV | Iteratively eliminate lowest-plurality candidate

explanations.text: That definition belongs to Dodgson, not Copeland. Copeland gives +1 per pairwise win and is Condorcet-consistent.
This is the Dodgson entry in the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Dodgson | Min swaps to become Condorcet winner; NP-hard

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match strategic and preference-structure terms from the key definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Condorcet winner: beats every other candidate head-to-head
Condorcet cycle: cyclical majority preferences
Strategyproof: no voter benefits from misreporting
Single-peaked: preferences decrease away from a peak on a line
Median-voter rule: elect the plurality winner among the two median-ranked candidates

explanations.text: This is the Condorcet winner entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Condorcet winner | Beats every other candidate head-to-head

explanations.text: This is the Condorcet cycle entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Condorcet cycle | Cyclical majority preferences

explanations.text: This is the strategyproofness entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Strategyproof | No voter benefits from misreporting

explanations.text: This is the single-peakedness entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Single-peaked | Preferences decrease away from peak on a line

explanations.text: The median-voter rule elects the median peak and is strategyproof for single-peaked preferences; it is not defined via plurality among two median-ranked candidates.

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section 10.2: Single-Winner Voting Rules; Section 10.3: Condorcet Principle
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly compare the main voting rules and principles from Week 10? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Plurality, Borda, veto, and $k$-approval are all positional scoring rules
No positional scoring rule is Condorcet-consistent for $m \geq 3$
IRV is Condorcet-consistent because it uses repeated majority comparisons
Copeland and maximin are presented as Condorcet-consistent methods
The worked examples show that plurality and IRV can both fail to elect the Condorcet winner

explanations.text: These four rules are grouped together under PSRs in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Borda ... Plurality ... Veto ... $k$-approval

explanations.text: This is one of the main theoretical results of the week.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3: Condorcet Consistency of Common Rules
explanations.notes_quote: No PSR is Condorcet-consistent (for $m \geq 3$)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly show IRV eliminating the Condorcet winner in the worked example.
Both are listed under Condorcet-consistent methods.
explanations.notes_quote: Copeland's rule ... Condorcet-consistent ... Maximin rule ... Condorcet-consistent

explanations.text: Plurality elects $a$ in the worked example even though $b$ beats $a$ head-to-head, and IRV eliminates $b$ despite $b$ being the Condorcet winner.
explanations.notes_quote: B ... beats a head-to-head 3-2 ... IRV eliminated b, who was the Condorcet winner

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem; Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly summarise manipulation, impossibility, and barriers from Week 10? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Gibbard-Satterthwaite implies that any non-dictatorial onto resolute rule with at least 3 candidates is manipulable
Single-peaked preferences provide a domain restriction under which strategyproof rules such as the median-voter rule become possible
Computational hardness of the manipulation problem can act as a barrier to manipulation
The notes conclude that all anonymous and neutral rules are strategyproof
Week 10 presents both structural barriers (single-peakedness) and computational barriers (NP-hard manipulation)

explanations.text: This is the main implication of Gibbard-Satterthwaite stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
explanations.notes_quote: Any non-dictatorial, onto voting rule with $m \geq 3$ is manipulable

explanations.text: The notes identify the median-voter rule as strategyproof under single-peaked preferences.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: Median-voter rule is strategyproof

explanations.text: This is the computational-barrier idea in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: If the $F$-manipulation problem ... is NP-hard, then $F$ is resistant to manipulation

explanations.text: The notes do not claim this; instead they present impossibility and manipulation results for broad classes of rules.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem

explanations.text: This captures the two kinds of barriers discussed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Strategic Manipulation (from LGT): Barriers to Manipulation
explanations.notes_quote: 1. Domain restriction -- Single-peaked preferences ... 2. Computational barriers
